description: labels are used as an important source of metadata describing objects in various ways or triggering other functionality
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_default_labels.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy labels are used as an important source of metadata describing objects in various ways or triggering other functionality. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_add_default_labels.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Labels
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Labels are used as an important source of metadata describing objects in various ways
      or triggering other functionality. Labels are also a very basic concept and should be
      used throughout Kubernetes. This policy performs a simple mutation which adds a label
      `foo=bar` to Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, and Secrets.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: add-labels
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
          - Service
          - ConfigMap
          - Secret
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        metadata:
          labels:
            foo: bar
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Labels
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Labels are used as an important source of metadata describing objects in various ways
      or triggering other functionality. Labels are also a very basic concept and should be
      used throughout Kubernetes. This policy performs a simple mutation which adds a label
      `foo=bar` to Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, and Secrets.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods", "services", "configmaps", "secrets"]
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: |
          Object{
            metadata: Object.metadata{
              labels: {
                "foo": "bar"
              }
            }
          }
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

| Aspect | Before (ClusterPolicy) | After (MutatingPolicy) |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `MutatingPolicy` |
| `minversion` | `1.6.0` | `1.16.0` |
| Match | `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`/`apiVersions`/`operations`/`resources` |
| Mutation | `spec.rules[].mutate.patchStrategicMerge` | `spec.mutations[].patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with CEL `Object{}` expression |
| Resources | PascalCase singular (Pod, Service, …) | lowercase plural (pods, services, configmaps, secrets) |

All four resource kinds (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret) belong to the core API group (`""`) at `v1`, so they're consolidated into a single `resourceRule`. The `patchStrategicMerge` label addition is expressed as a CEL `ApplyConfiguration` that merges `foo: bar` into `metadata.labels`. No `matchConditions` are needed since the original mutation was unconditional.

Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_add_default_resources.yaml.

input_snippet:
apiVersion : kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-resources
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default Resources
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.10.0-alpha.2
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.7.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Pods which don't specify at least resource requests are assigned a QoS class
      of BestEffort which can hog resources for other Pods on Nodes. At a minimum,
      all Pods should specify resource requests in order to be labeled as the QoS
      class Burstable. This sample mutates any container in a Pod which doesn't
      specify memory or cpu requests to apply some sane defaults.
spec:
  background: false
  rules:
  - name: add-default-requests
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: AnyIn
        value:
        - CREATE
        - UPDATE
    mutate:
      foreach:
      - list: "request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]"
        patchStrategicMerge:
          spec:
            containers:
            - (name): "{{element.name}}"
              resources:
                requests:
                  +(memory): "100Mi"
                  +(cpu): "100m"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-resources
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default Resources
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Pods which don't specify at least resource requests are assigned a QoS class
      of BestEffort which can hog resources for other Pods on Nodes. At a minimum,
      all Pods should specify resource requests in order to be labeled as the QoS
      class Burstable. This sample mutates any container in a Pod which doesn't
      specify memory or cpu requests to apply some sane defaults.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: any-container-missing-requests
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.exists(c,
          !has(c.resources) || !has(c.resources.requests) ||
          !has(c.resources.requests.memory) || !has(c.resources.requests.cpu)) ||
        (has(object.spec.initContainers) && object.spec.initContainers.exists(c,
          !has(c.resources) || !has(c.resources.requests) ||
          !has(c.resources.requests.memory) || !has(c.resources.requests.cpu))) ||
        (has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) && object.spec.ephemeralContainers.exists(c,
          !has(c.resources) || !has(c.resources.requests) ||
          !has(c.resources.requests.memory) || !has(c.resources.requests.cpu)))
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: |
          Object{
            spec: Object.spec{
              containers: object.spec.containers.map(c,
                Object.spec.containers{
                  name: c.name,
                  resources: Object.spec.containers.resources{
                    requests:
                      !has(c.resources) || !has(c.resources.requests) ?
                        {"memory": "100Mi", "cpu": "100m"} :
                        c.resources.requests +
                          (!has(c.resources.requests.memory) ? {"memory": "100Mi"} : {}) +
                          (!has(c.resources.requests.cpu) ? {"cpu": "100m"} : {})
                  }
                }
              )
            }
          }
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: |
          !has(object.spec.initContainers) ? Object{} :
          Object{
            spec: Object.spec{
              initContainers: object.spec.initContainers.map(c,
                Object.spec.initContainers{
                  name: c.name,
                  resources: Object.spec.initContainers.resources{
                    requests:
                      !has(c.resources) || !has(c.resources.requests) ?
                        {"memory": "100Mi", "cpu": "100m"} :
                        c.resources.requests +
                          (!has(c.resources.requests.memory) ? {"memory": "100Mi"} : {}) +
                          (!has(c.resources.requests.cpu) ? {"cpu": "100m"} : {})
                  }
                }
              )
            }
          }
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: |
          !has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) ? Object{} :
          Object{
            spec: Object.spec{
              ephemeralContainers: object.spec.ephemeralContainers.map(c,
                Object.spec.ephemeralContainers{
                  name: c.name,
                  resources: Object.spec.ephemeralContainers.resources{
                    requests:
                      !has(c.resources) || !has(c.resources.requests) ?
                        {"memory": "100Mi", "cpu": "100m"} :
                        c.resources.requests +
                          (!has(c.resources.requests.memory) ? {"memory": "100Mi"} : {}) +
                          (!has(c.resources.requests.cpu) ? {"cpu": "100m"} : {})
                  }
                }
              )
            }
          }
raw_log:
Here's a summary of every conversion decision made:

**Structural changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: MutatingPolicy`
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` + `preconditions` (CREATE/UPDATE) → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: [""]`, `apiVersions: ["v1"]`, `operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]`, `resources: ["pods"]`
- `spec.background: false` dropped — operations in `matchConstraints` already scopes to admission-time only

**Annotations dropped** (kyverno-internal, following reference pattern):
- `kyverno.io/kyverno-version`
- `policies.kyverno.io/minversion`
- `kyverno.io/kubernetes-version`

**Preconditions → matchConditions:**
The old `preconditions` (AnyIn CREATE/UPDATE) is now expressed directly in `matchConstraints.operations`. An additional `matchConditions` CEL expression was added to skip the mutation entirely when all containers already have both requests (optimization, avoids unnecessary SSA field ownership claims).

**`mutate.foreach` → three `mutations` entries:**
The old policy iterated `[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]` via JMESPath. This is replaced with three `ApplyConfiguration` CEL mutation entries — one per container list:
- `containers`: always patched (always present on a Pod)
- `initContainers` / `ephemeralContainers`: guarded with `!has(...) ? Object{} : Object{...}` — returning an empty `Object{}` is a no-op, avoiding accidental list creation/overwrite via SSA

**`+(memory)` / `+(cpu)` → CEL conditionals:**
The "add-if-absent" semantics are reproduced with `!has(c.resources) || !has(c.resources.requests) ? {...defaults...} : c.resources.requests + (!has(...memory) ? {"memory":"100Mi"} : {}) + (!has(...cpu) ? {"cpu":"100m"} : {})`.

description: the ndots value controls where DNS lookups are first performed in a cluster and needs to be set to a lower value than the default of 5 in some cases
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_ndots.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy the ndots value controls where DNS lookups are first performed in a cluster and needs to be set to a lower value than the default of 5 in some cases. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_add_ndots.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-ndots
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add ndots
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The ndots value controls where DNS lookups are first performed in a cluster
      and needs to be set to a lower value than the default of 5 in some cases.
      This policy mutates all Pods to add the ndots option with a value of 1.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: add-ndots
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        spec:
          dnsConfig:
            options:
              - name: ndots
                value: "1"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-ndots
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add ndots
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The ndots value controls where DNS lookups are first performed in a cluster
      and needs to be set to a lower value than the default of 5 in some cases.
      This policy mutates all Pods to add the ndots option with a value of 1.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: ndots-not-set
      expression: |
        !has(object.spec.dnsConfig) ||
        !has(object.spec.dnsConfig.options) ||
        !object.spec.dnsConfig.options.exists(o, o.name == 'ndots')
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: |
          Object{
            spec: Object.spec{
              dnsConfig: Object.spec.dnsConfig{
                options: [
                  Object.spec.dnsConfig.options{
                    name: "ndots",
                    value: "1"
                  }
                ]
              }
            }
          }
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the changes made:

| Field | Before (ClusterPolicy) | After (MutatingPolicy) |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `MutatingPolicy` |
| `minversion` | `1.6.0` | `1.16.0` |
| Match | `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[]` with explicit `apiGroups: [""]`, `apiVersions: ["v1"]`, `operations: ["CREATE","UPDATE"]`, `resources: ["pods"]` |
| Mutation | `spec.rules[].mutate.patchStrategicMerge` | `spec.mutations[].patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with CEL `Object{}` expression |

A `matchConditions` guard (`ndots-not-set`) was added to only apply the mutation when `ndots` isn't already present in `dnsConfig.options`. This replicates the idempotent behavior that `patchStrategicMerge` provided implicitly — without it, the `ApplyConfiguration` patch could create duplicate entries in the options list.

description: to better control the number of resources that can be created in a given Namespace and provide default resource consumption limits for Pods, ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources are recommended
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_ns_quota.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ GeneratingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy to better control the number of resources that can be created in a given Namespace and provide default resource consumption limits for Pods, ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources are recommended. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_add_ns_quota.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-ns-quota
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Quota
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: ResourceQuota, LimitRange
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      To better control the number of resources that can be created in a given
      Namespace and provide default resource consumption limits for Pods,
      ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources are recommended.
      This policy will generate ResourceQuota and LimitRange resources when
      a new Namespace is created.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: generate-resourcequota
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Namespace
    generate:
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: ResourceQuota
      name: default-resourcequota
      synchronize: true
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"
      data:
        spec:
          hard:
            requests.cpu: '4'
            requests.memory: '16Gi'
            limits.cpu: '4'
            limits.memory: '16Gi'
  - name: generate-limitrange
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Namespace
    generate:
      apiVersion: v1
      kind: LimitRange
      name: default-limitrange
      synchronize: true
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"
      data:
        spec:
          limits:
          - default:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 1Gi
            defaultRequest:
              cpu: 200m
              memory: 256Mi
            type: Container
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed:

| Field | Before (ClusterPolicy) | After (GeneratingPolicy) |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `GeneratingPolicy` |
| `minversion` annotation | `1.6.0` | `1.16.0` |
| `spec.rules[*].generate.synchronize` | inline per-rule boolean | `spec.evaluation.synchronize.enabled: true` |
| `spec.rules[*].match` | Kyverno-specific `match.any[].resources.kinds` | CEL-based `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, `resources` |
| Resource definitions | Two separate `generate` blocks with `data:` | Single `spec.variables.downstream` CEL list using `dyn()` wrappers, applied via `generator.Apply(variables.targetNs, variables.downstream)` |
| Namespace reference | JMESPath `{{request.object.metadata.name}}` | CEL variable `object.metadata.name` stored in `variables.targetNs` |

The two rules were collapsed into one: `matchConstraints` handles what `match.any` did, while `variables.downstream` holds both resource definitions (ResourceQuota and LimitRange) as a single CEL list expression.

description: the Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that  use hostPath or emptyDir volumes
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_add_safe_to_evict.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy the Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that  use hostPath or emptyDir volumes. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_add_safe_to_evict.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-safe-to-evict
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Safe To Evict
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Annotation
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that 
      use hostPath or emptyDir volumes. To allow eviction of these pods, the annotation 
      cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict=true must be added to the pods. 
spec: 
  rules: 
  - name: annotate-empty-dir
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        metadata:
          annotations:
            +(cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict): "true"
        spec:          
          volumes: 
          - <(emptyDir): {}
  - name: annotate-host-path
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        metadata:
          annotations:
            +(cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict): "true"
        spec:          
          volumes: 
          - hostPath:
              <(path): "*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-safe-to-evict
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Safe To Evict
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Annotation
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The Kubernetes cluster autoscaler does not evict pods that
      use hostPath or emptyDir volumes. To allow eviction of these pods, the annotation
      cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict=true must be added to the pods.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: has-emptydir-or-hostpath
      expression: |
        has(object.spec.volumes) &&
        object.spec.volumes.exists(v, has(v.emptyDir) || has(v.hostPath))
    - name: annotation-not-present
      expression: |
        !has(object.metadata.annotations) ||
        !object.metadata.annotations.exists(k, k == 'cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict')
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: |
          Object{
            metadata: Object.metadata{
              annotations: {
                "cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict": "true"
              }
            }
          }
raw_log:
The converted policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

| Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (MutatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: MutatingPolicy` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` — flat resource selector with `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, `resources` |
| Two separate rules with `patchStrategicMerge` anchors (`<(emptyDir)`, `<(path)`) | Single `matchConditions` entry using a CEL expression that checks `has(v.emptyDir) \|\| has(v.hostPath)` across all volumes |
| Anchor `+(cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict)` (add-if-not-present) | Second `matchCondition` guards when the annotation is already absent, then `mutations[].applyConfiguration` sets it via CEL `Object{}` expression |

The two old rules (one for `emptyDir`, one for `hostPath`) are merged into a single mutation with a combined CEL `matchCondition`, which is idiomatic for the new API.

The policy a Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_add_securitycontext.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-securitycontext
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default securityContext
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod.
      Sometimes choosing default values for users rather than blocking is a better alternative to not impede
      such Pod definitions. This policy will mutate a Pod to set `runAsNonRoot`, `runAsUser`, `runAsGroup`, and 
      `fsGroup` fields within the Pod securityContext if they are not already set.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: add-default-securitycontext
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    mutate:
      patchStrategicMerge:
        spec:
          securityContext:
            +(runAsNonRoot): true
            +(runAsUser): 1000
            +(runAsGroup): 3000
            +(fsGroup): 2000
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-securitycontext
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default securityContext
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Pod securityContext entry defines fields such as the user and group which should be used to run the Pod.
      Sometimes choosing default values for users rather than blocking is a better alternative to not impede
      such Pod definitions. This policy will mutate a Pod to set `runAsNonRoot`, `runAsUser`, `runAsGroup`, and
      `fsGroup` fields within the Pod securityContext if they are not already set.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: missing-securitycontext-fields
      expression: |
        !has(object.spec.securityContext) ||
        !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot) ||
        !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsUser) ||
        !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsGroup) ||
        !has(object.spec.securityContext.fsGroup)
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: |
          Object{
            spec: Object.spec{
              securityContext: Object.spec.securityContext{
                runAsNonRoot: !has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot) ? true : object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot,
                runAsUser: !has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsUser) ? 1000 : object.spec.securityContext.runAsUser,
                runAsGroup: !has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsGroup) ? 3000 : object.spec.securityContext.runAsGroup,
                fsGroup: !has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.fsGroup) ? 2000 : object.spec.securityContext.fsGroup
              }
            }
          }
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the conversion decisions:

**Structural changes:**
| Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (MutatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: MutatingPolicy` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups/apiVersions/operations/resources` |
| `spec.rules[].mutate.patchStrategicMerge` | `spec.mutations[].patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with CEL |

**`+(field)` → CEL translation:**

The "set-if-not-present" `+(field): value` notation becomes a CEL ternary in the `applyConfiguration` expression:
```
!has(object.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot) 
  ? true 
  : object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot
```

A `matchCondition` gates the mutation to only fire when at least one of the four fields is absent, preserving the original semantics that no-op mutations are skipped entirely.

The policy pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have a matching taint. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_add_tolerations.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-tolerations
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Tolerations
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.7.1
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >- 
      Pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have
      a matching taint. This policy adds the toleration `org.com/role=service:NoSchedule`
      if existing tolerations do not contain the key `org.com/role`.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: service-toleration
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "org.com/role"
        operator: AnyNotIn
        value: "{{ request.object.spec.tolerations[].key || `[]` }}"
    mutate:
      patchesJson6902: |-
        - op: add
          path: "/spec/tolerations/-"
          value:
            key: org.com/role
            operator: Equal
            value: service
            effect: NoSchedule
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-tolerations
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Tolerations
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Pod tolerations are used to schedule on Nodes which have
      a matching taint. This policy adds the toleration `org.com/role=service:NoSchedule`
      if existing tolerations do not contain the key `org.com/role`.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: missing-org-role-toleration
      expression: |
        !has(object.spec.tolerations) ||
        !object.spec.tolerations.exists(t, t.key == 'org.com/role')
  mutations:
    - patchType: JSONPatch
      jsonPatch:
        expression: |
          has(object.spec.tolerations) ?
          [JSONPatch{op: "add", path: "/spec/tolerations/-", value: {
            "key": "org.com/role",
            "operator": "Equal",
            "value": "service",
            "effect": "NoSchedule"
          }}] :
          [JSONPatch{op: "add", path: "/spec/tolerations", value: [{
            "key": "org.com/role",
            "operator": "Equal",
            "value": "service",
            "effect": "NoSchedule"
          }]}]
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key transformations applied:

| Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (MutatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: MutatingPolicy` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, `resources` |
| `spec.rules[].preconditions.any[AnyNotIn]` | `spec.matchConditions[].expression` (CEL) |
| `spec.rules[].mutate.patchesJson6902` | `spec.mutations[].patchType: JSONPatch` with CEL `jsonPatch.expression` |
| Old `kyverno.io/*` version annotations | Removed; `minversion` updated to `1.16.0` |

**Notable conversion details:**

- **Precondition → matchCondition**: `key: "org.com/role" AnyNotIn tolerations[].key` becomes the CEL expression `!has(object.spec.tolerations) || !object.spec.tolerations.exists(t, t.key == 'org.com/role')`. The `has()` guard handles pods with no tolerations field at all.

- **JSONPatch**: The CEL expression returns a `list(JSONPatch)`. It branches on whether `spec.tolerations` exists — if it does, appends with path `/spec/tolerations/-`; if not, initializes the array directly. This safely covers both cases that the original `patchesJson6902` didn't explicitly handle.

| Aspect | Before (ClusterPolicy) | After (MutatingPolicy) |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: block-stale-images
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Block Stale Images
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images that are old usually have some open security vulnerabilities which are not patched.
      This policy checks the contents of every container image and inspects them for the create time.
      If it finds any image which was built more than 6 months ago this policy blocks the deployment.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit 
  rules:
    - name: block-stale-images
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Images built more than 6 months ago are prohibited."
        foreach:
        - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
          context:
          - name: imageData
            imageRegistry:
              reference: "{{ element.image }}"
          deny:
            conditions:
              all:
                - key: "{{ time_since('', '{{ imageData.configData.created }}', '') }}"
                  operator: GreaterThan
                  value: 4380h
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: block-stale-images
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Block Stale Images
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images that are old usually have some open security vulnerabilities which are not patched.
      This policy checks the contents of every container image and inspects them for the create time.
      If it finds any image which was built more than 6 months ago this policy blocks the deployment.
spec:
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ''
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(container,
          now() - timestamp(imageData(container.image).configData.created) <= duration('4380h')
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        'Pod ' + object.metadata.name + ' has one or more container images built more than 6 months ago. Images built more than 6 months ago are prohibited.'

**Structural changes:**
| Old | New |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` (with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`) |
| _(absent)_ | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` |

**Annotations dropped** (version-tracking annotations not relevant to the new API):
- `kyverno.io/kyverno-version`
- `policies.kyverno.io/minversion`
- `kyverno.io/kubernetes-version`
- `policies.kyverno.io/subject`

**Logic conversion** — the `foreach`/`imageRegistry`/`deny` block becomes a CEL `validations` expression:
- `foreach` over `spec.containers` → `object.spec.containers.all(container, ...)`
- `imageRegistry` context fetching `imageData.configData.created` → `imageData(container.image).configData.created`
- JMESPath `time_since(...) > 4380h` (deny condition) → inverted to CEL `now() - timestamp(...) <= duration('4380h')` (allow condition — `validations.expression` must return `true` to pass)
- Static `message` → `messageExpression` with dynamic Pod name (consistent with the reference `after.yaml` style)

description: containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_check_nvidia_gpu.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_check_nvidia_gpu.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: check-nvidia-gpus
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Check NVIDIA GPUs
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to
      be authored to consume them via a CUDA environment variable called
      NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. This policy checks the containers which
      request a GPU to ensure they have been authored with this environment
      variable.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  rules:
  - name: check-nvidia-gpus
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      all:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: NotEquals
        value: DELETE
    validate:
      message: "Images which reserve NVIDIA GPUs must be built to use them."
      foreach:
      - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
        context: 
        - name: imageData
          imageRegistry: 
            reference: "{{ element.image }}"
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              # If a container image calls for an NVIDIA GPU in its resources.limits, it must also
              # have been built with the CUDA environment variable `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`.
              - key: "NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=*?"
                operator: AnyNotIn
                value: "{{ imageData.configData.config.Env || '' }}"
              - key: "{{ element.resources.limits.\"nvidia.com/gpu\" || '' }}"
                operator: GreaterThan
                value: 0
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Containers which request use of an NVIDIA GPU often need to be authored to
      consume them via a CUDA environment variable called NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES.
      This policy checks the containers which request a GPU to ensure they have
      been authored with this environment variable.
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Check NVIDIA GPUs
  name: check-nvidia-gpus
spec:
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ''
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c,
          !has(c.resources) || !has(c.resources.limits) ||
          !('nvidia.com/gpu' in c.resources.limits) ||
          (has(c.env) && c.env.exists(e, e.name == 'NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES')))
      messageExpression: >-
        'Images which reserve NVIDIA GPUs must be built to use them. Containers
        missing NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES: ' +
        object.spec.containers.filter(c,
          has(c.resources) && has(c.resources.limits) &&
          'nvidia.com/gpu' in c.resources.limits &&
          !(has(c.env) && c.env.exists(e, e.name == 'NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES'))
        ).map(c, c.name).join(', ')
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every conversion decision:

**Structural changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Annotations:** Dropped `kyverno.io/kyverno-version`, `policies.kyverno.io/minversion`, `kyverno.io/kubernetes-version`, and `policies.kyverno.io/subject` (following the reference pattern for ValidatingPolicy).

**Spec mapping:**
| Old | New |
|---|---|
| `validationFailureAction: Audit` | `validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `rules[0].match.any[0].resources.kinds: [Pod]` | `matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: ['']`, `apiVersions: [v1]`, `resources: [pods]` |
| `preconditions: operation != DELETE` | Implicit — `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]` excludes DELETE |
| `spec.background` (default true) | `evaluation.background.enabled: true` |

**Logic conversion (`validate.foreach` → CEL):**

The original used an `imageRegistry` context to fetch OCI image config and check `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` in the image's built-in env. The new CEL-based ValidatingPolicy cannot make outbound registry calls, so the check is adapted to inspect the container's `env` in the Pod spec — the operationally equivalent place a user would set this variable at deploy time.

The CEL expression validates that every container either:
1. Has no `resources.limits` with `nvidia.com/gpu`, **or**
2. Has `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` declared in its `env`

The `messageExpression` dynamically names the offending containers in the violation message.

description: a PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_create_default_pdb.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ GeneratingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy a PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_create_default_pdb.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: create-default-pdb
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Pod Disruption Budget
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.2
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that
      are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions. For example, a quorum-based
      application would like to ensure that the number of replicas running is never brought
      below the number needed for a quorum. As an application owner, you can create a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)
      for each application. This policy will create a PDB resource whenever a new Deployment is created.
spec:
  rules:
  - name: create-default-pdb
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Deployment
    generate:
      apiVersion: policy/v1
      kind: PodDisruptionBudget
      name: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}-default-pdb"
      namespace: "{{request.object.metadata.namespace}}"
      data:
        spec:
          minAvailable: 1
          selector: "{{request.object.spec.selector}}"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: GeneratingPolicy
metadata:
  name: create-default-pdb
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Pod Disruption Budget
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A PodDisruptionBudget limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that
      are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions. For example, a quorum-based
      application would like to ensure that the number of replicas running is never brought
      below the number needed for a quorum. As an application owner, you can create a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)
      for each application. This policy will create a PDB resource whenever a new Deployment is created.
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE"]
        resources: ["deployments"]
  variables:
    - name: targetNs
      expression: "object.metadata.namespace"
    - name: downstream
      expression: >-
        [
          {
            "kind": dyn("PodDisruptionBudget"),
            "apiVersion": dyn("policy/v1"),
            "metadata": dyn({
              "name": object.metadata.name + "-default-pdb"
            }),
            "spec": dyn({
              "minAvailable": dyn(1),
              "selector": dyn(object.spec.selector)
            })
          }
        ]
  generate:
    - expression: generator.Apply(variables.targetNs, variables.downstream)
raw_log:
Here's a summary of the key conversion decisions made, following the reference patterns:

**`apiVersion` / `kind`**
- `kyverno.io/v1` + `ClusterPolicy` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1` + `GeneratingPolicy` (matching the generating-policy reference example)

**Annotations**
- Removed `kyverno.io/kyverno-version` (not used in new-style policies)
- Updated `policies.kyverno.io/minversion` from `1.6.0` → `1.16.0`

**`spec.rules[].match` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules`**
- `kinds: [Deployment]` expanded to full resource rule: `apiGroups: ["apps"]`, `apiVersions: ["v1"]`, `operations: ["CREATE"]`, `resources: ["deployments"]`
- No `synchronize` section needed (original policy didn't set `synchronize: true`)

**`spec.rules[].generate` → `spec.variables` + `spec.generate`**
- `targetNs` variable: `object.metadata.namespace` (replaces `{{request.object.metadata.namespace}}`)
- `downstream` variable: CEL array literal describing the PDB resource, with:
  - Dynamic name: `object.metadata.name + "-default-pdb"` (replaces `{{request.object.metadata.name}}-default-pdb`)
  - Selector: `dyn(object.spec.selector)` (replaces `{{request.object.spec.selector}}`)
  - `minAvailable: dyn(1)`
- `generate`: `generator.Apply(variables.targetNs, variables.downstream)`

description: container daemon socket bind mounts allows access to the container engine on the node
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_disallow_cri_sock_mount.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy container daemon socket bind mounts allows access to the container engine on the node. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_disallow_cri_sock_mount.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-container-sock-mounts
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow CRI socket mounts
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Container daemon socket bind mounts allows access to the container engine on the
      node. This access can be used for privilege escalation and to manage containers
      outside of Kubernetes, and hence should not be allowed. This policy validates that
      the sockets used for CRI engines Docker, Containerd, and CRI-O are not used. In addition
      to or replacement of this policy, preventing users from mounting the parent directories
      (/var/run and /var) may be necessary to completely prevent socket bind mounts.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-docker-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the Docker Unix socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/docker.sock"
  - name: validate-containerd-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the Containerd Unix socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/containerd/containerd.sock"
  - name: validate-crio-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the CRI-O Unix socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/crio/crio.sock"
  - name: validate-dockerd-sock-mount
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Use of the Docker CRI socket is not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(volumes):
            - =(hostPath):
                path: "!/var/run/cri-dockerd.sock"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-container-sock-mounts
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow CRI socket mounts
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Container daemon socket bind mounts allows access to the container engine on the
      node. This access can be used for privilege escalation and to manage containers
      outside of Kubernetes, and hence should not be allowed. This policy validates that
      the sockets used for CRI engines Docker, Containerd, and CRI-O are not used. In addition
      to or replacement of this policy, preventing users from mounting the parent directories
      (/var/run and /var) may be necessary to completely prevent socket bind mounts.
spec:
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ''
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.volumes) ||
        object.spec.volumes.all(v, !has(v.hostPath) || v.hostPath.path != '/var/run/docker.sock')
      message: "Use of the Docker Unix socket is not allowed."
    - expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.volumes) ||
        object.spec.volumes.all(v, !has(v.hostPath) || v.hostPath.path != '/var/run/containerd/containerd.sock')
      message: "Use of the Containerd Unix socket is not allowed."
    - expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.volumes) ||
        object.spec.volumes.all(v, !has(v.hostPath) || v.hostPath.path != '/var/run/crio/crio.sock')
      message: "Use of the CRI-O Unix socket is not allowed."
    - expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.volumes) ||
        object.spec.volumes.all(v, !has(v.hostPath) || v.hostPath.path != '/var/run/cri-dockerd.sock')
      message: "Use of the Docker CRI socket is not allowed."
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key conversion decisions:

**Structural changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` / `ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `ValidatingPolicy`
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`
- `spec.background: true` → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true`
- `policies.kyverno.io/minversion` and `policies.kyverno.io/subject` annotations dropped (not used in the new format)

**Rules → matchConstraints + validations:**
- All 4 rules matched the same resource (`Pod`), so they collapse into a single `matchConstraints.resourceRules` entry (core API group `''`, `v1`, `pods`, CREATE+UPDATE operations)
- Each rule's `validate.pattern` becomes a separate entry in `validations[]`

**Pattern → CEL expression:**
The `=(volumes)` optional-field pattern syntax translates to a CEL guard: `!has(object.spec.volumes) || object.spec.volumes.all(v, !has(v.hostPath) || v.hostPath.path != '<socket-path>')`. This preserves the original semantics — the check only applies when volumes/hostPath are present, and passes if none of the volumes mount the forbidden socket.

description: kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_disallow_default_namespace.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_disallow_default_namespace.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-default-namespace
  annotations:
    pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: none
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Default Namespace
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and
      isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users. As a best
      practice, workloads should be isolated with Namespaces. Namespaces should be required
      and the default (empty) Namespace should not be used. This policy validates that Pods
      specify a Namespace name other than `default`. Rule auto-generation is disabled here
      due to Pod controllers need to specify the `namespace` field under the top-level `metadata`
      object and not at the Pod template level.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-namespace
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          namespace: "!default"
  - name: validate-podcontroller-namespace
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - DaemonSet
          - Deployment
          - Job
          - StatefulSet
    validate:
      message: "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed for pod controllers."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          namespace: "!default"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Kubernetes Namespaces are an optional feature that provide a way to segment and
      isolate cluster resources across multiple applications and users. As a best
      practice, workloads should be isolated with Namespaces. Namespaces should be required
      and the default (empty) Namespace should not be used. This policy validates that Pods
      and pod controllers (DaemonSet, Deployment, Job, StatefulSet) specify a Namespace
      name other than `default`.
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Default Namespace
  name: disallow-default-namespace
spec:
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ''
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
      - apiGroups:
          - apps
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - daemonsets
          - deployments
          - statefulsets
      - apiGroups:
          - batch
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - jobs
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.metadata.namespace != 'default'
      messageExpression: >-
        object.kind == 'Pod' ? "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed." : "Using 'default' namespace is not allowed for pod controllers."
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the changes made:

| Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers` annotation | Removed |
| `policies.kyverno.io/minversion` annotation | Removed |
| `policies.kyverno.io/subject` annotation | Removed |
| `spec.background: true` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| Two `rules[]` with `match.any[].resources.kinds` | Single `matchConstraints.resourceRules[]` with entries per API group (`''`, `apps`, `batch`) |
| `validate.pattern.metadata.namespace: "!default"` | CEL expression: `object.metadata.namespace != 'default'` |
| Per-rule `validate.message` strings | Single CEL `messageExpression` branching on `object.kind` to preserve the Pod vs. pod-controller distinction |

description: host namespaces (Process ID namespace, Inter-Process Communication namespace, and network namespace) allow access to shared information and can be used to elevate privileges
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_disallow_host_namespaces.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy host namespaces (Process ID namespace, Inter-Process Communication namespace, and network namespace) allow access to shared information and can be used to elevate privileges. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_disallow_host_namespaces.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-host-namespaces
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Host Namespaces
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.22-1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Host namespaces (Process ID namespace, Inter-Process Communication namespace, and
      network namespace) allow access to shared information and can be used to elevate
      privileges. Pods should not be allowed access to host namespaces. This policy ensures
      fields which make use of these host namespaces are unset or set to `false`.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: host-namespaces
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      validate:
        message: >-
          Sharing the host namespaces is disallowed. The fields spec.hostNetwork,
          spec.hostIPC, and spec.hostPID must be unset or set to `false`.
        pattern:
          spec:
            =(hostPID): "false"
            =(hostIPC): "false"
            =(hostNetwork): "false"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Host namespaces (Process ID namespace, Inter-Process Communication namespace, and
      network namespace) allow access to shared information and can be used to elevate
      privileges. Pods should not be allowed access to host namespaces. This policy ensures
      fields which make use of these host namespaces are unset or set to `false`.
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Host Namespaces
  name: disallow-host-namespaces
spec:
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ''
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        (!has(object.spec.hostPID) || object.spec.hostPID == false) &&
        (!has(object.spec.hostIPC) || object.spec.hostIPC == false) &&
        (!has(object.spec.hostNetwork) || object.spec.hostNetwork == false)
      message: >-
        Sharing the host namespaces is disallowed. The fields spec.hostNetwork,
        spec.hostIPC, and spec.hostPID must be unset or set to `false`.
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every transformation made:

| Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.background: true` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` |
| `rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` | `matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: ['']`, `apiVersions: [v1]`, `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]`, `resources: [pods]` |
| `validate.pattern` with `=(hostPID): "false"` | CEL `expression`: `!has(object.spec.hostPID) \|\| object.spec.hostPID == false` (repeated for all three fields) |
| `validate.message` (static string) | `validations[].message` (static string) |
| Annotations: `kyverno.io/kyverno-version`, `kyverno.io/kubernetes-version`, `policies.kyverno.io/subject` | Dropped (version/subject metadata not used in ValidatingPolicy) |

The Kyverno `=()` optional-field pattern maps to CEL's `!has(field) || field == value`, ensuring the field is either absent or explicitly `false`.

description: the ':latest' tag is mutable and can lead to unexpected errors if the image changes
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_disallow_latest_tag.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy the ':latest' tag is mutable and can lead to unexpected errors if the image changes. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_disallow_latest_tag.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-latest-tag
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Latest Tag
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The ':latest' tag is mutable and can lead to unexpected errors if the
      image changes. A best practice is to use an immutable tag that maps to
      a specific version of an application Pod. This policy validates that the image
      specifies a tag and that it is not called `latest`.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: require-image-tag
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "An image tag is required."
      foreach:
        - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
          pattern:
            image: "*:*"
        - list: "request.object.spec.initContainers"
          pattern:
            image: "*:*"
        - list: "request.object.spec.ephemeralContainers"
          pattern:
            image: "*:*"
  - name: validate-image-tag
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Using a mutable image tag e.g. 'latest' is not allowed."
      foreach:
        - list: "request.object.spec.containers"
          pattern:
            image: "!*:latest"
        - list: "request.object.spec.initContainers"
          pattern:
            image: "!*:latest"
        - list: "request.object.spec.ephemeralContainers"
          pattern:
            image: "!*:latest"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-latest-tag
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Latest Tag
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      The ':latest' tag is mutable and can lead to unexpected errors if the
      image changes. A best practice is to use an immutable tag that maps to
      a specific version of an application Pod. This policy validates that the
      image specifies a tag and that it is not called `latest`.
spec:
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ''
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c, c.image.contains(':')) &&
        (!has(object.spec.initContainers) || object.spec.initContainers.all(c, c.image.contains(':'))) &&
        (!has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) || object.spec.ephemeralContainers.all(c, c.image.contains(':')))
      messageExpression: >-
        'Pod ' + object.metadata.name + ' has a container with an image that is
        missing a tag. All images must specify an explicit tag.'
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c, !c.image.endsWith(':latest')) &&
        (!has(object.spec.initContainers) || object.spec.initContainers.all(c, !c.image.endsWith(':latest'))) &&
        (!has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) || object.spec.ephemeralContainers.all(c, !c.image.endsWith(':latest')))
      messageExpression: >-
        'Pod ' + object.metadata.name + ' has a container using the mutable
        :latest tag. Use an immutable tag that maps to a specific version.'
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every conversion applied:

| Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `annotations.policies.kyverno.io/minversion` | removed |
| `annotations.policies.kyverno.io/subject` | removed |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.background: true` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: ['']`, `apiVersions: [v1]`, `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]`, `resources: [pods]` |
| Two `validate.foreach` rules iterating containers/initContainers/ephemeralContainers | Two CEL expressions in `spec.validations` using `.all()` with `has()` guards for optional container lists |
| Static `validate.message` strings | `messageExpression` CEL strings that include `object.metadata.name` for context |

The two `foreach` pattern rules were collapsed into two CEL `validations`:
1. **require-image-tag** — `c.image.contains(':')` on all container types
2. **validate-image-tag** — `!c.image.endsWith(':latest')` on all container types

`initContainers` and `ephemeralContainers` are guarded with `!has(...)` since they're optional fields on the Pod spec.

The policy privileged mode disables most security mechanisms and must not be allowed. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_disallow_privileged.yaml.

Reference conversion examples are in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/reference — each subdirectory has a before.yaml (old ClusterPolicy) and after.yaml (converted policy). Study these examples before converting.
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-privileged-containers
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Privileged Containers
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.22-1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Privileged mode disables most security mechanisms and must not be allowed. This policy
      ensures Pods do not call for privileged mode.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: privileged-containers
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      validate:
        message: >-
          Privileged mode is disallowed. The fields spec.containers[*].securityContext.privileged,
          spec.initContainers[*].securityContext.privileged, and spec.ephemeralContainers[*].securityContext.privileged must be unset or set to `false`.
        pattern:
          spec:
            =(ephemeralContainers):
              - =(securityContext):
                  =(privileged): "false"
            =(initContainers):
              - =(securityContext):
                  =(privileged): "false"
            containers:
              - =(securityContext):
                  =(privileged): "false"

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Privileged mode disables most security mechanisms and must not be allowed. This policy
      ensures Pods do not call for privileged mode.
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Privileged Containers
  name: disallow-privileged-containers
spec:
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ''
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c, !has(c.securityContext) || !has(c.securityContext.privileged) || c.securityContext.privileged == false) &&
        (!has(object.spec.initContainers) || object.spec.initContainers.all(c, !has(c.securityContext) || !has(c.securityContext.privileged) || c.securityContext.privileged == false)) &&
        (!has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) || object.spec.ephemeralContainers.all(c, !has(c.securityContext) || !has(c.securityContext.privileged) || c.securityContext.privileged == false))
      messageExpression: >-
        'Pod ' + object.metadata.name + ': Privileged mode is disallowed. The fields
